description: A statement cannot be both true and false — and we can prove it.

guides.text: The goal is $\lnot (A \land \lnot A)$. This is the **law of non-[contradiction|contradiction]**: no [proposition|proposition] can be both true and false at the same time. To prove a [negation|negation], we use **[reductio-ad-absurdum|reductio ad absurdum]** — assume the thing we want to disprove, and show it leads to a contradiction.

The strategy: assume $A \land \lnot A$, then demonstrate that it contains two contradictory statements — $A$ and $\lnot A$. The rule `not-intro` will then conclude $\lnot (A \land \lnot A)$ for us. First, make the [assumption|assumption].
And the right conjunct: $\lnot A$. Now you hold both $A$ (step 2) and $\lnot A$ (step 3) — a [contradiction|contradiction].

The rule `not-intro` takes three arguments: the [formula|formula] to negate, and two steps that (a) both have that formula in their [context|context], and (b) prove contradictory things. Steps 2 and 3 share $A \land \lnot A$ in their context, and their [conclusion|conclusions] are $A$ and $\lnot A$ — perfect. The result discharges the [assumption|assumption] and yields its [negation|negation].
That's it. No [assumption|assumptions] remain — the law of non-[contradiction|contradiction] stands on its own as an unconditional truth.

unlocks.name: ¬-intro
unlocks.description: If assuming $p$ leads to both $q$ and $\lnot q$ (a contradiction), then $\lnot p$ must hold. *Reductio ad absurdum* — reduce to the absurd.
